ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general cuttlefish food product entry; no specific stir-fried cuttlefish with chilli entry exists. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers cooked cuttlefish as the closest defensible match; no specific stir-fried chilli preparation code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for stir-fried cuttlefish with chilli; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of stir-fried cuttlefish with chilli is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one small plate as commonly served at Singaporean hawker centres and zi char stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Stir Fried Cuttlefish with Chilli — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for Stir Fried Cuttlefish with Chilli (cr_id F03060035). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical source. Saturated fat, potassium, and cholesterol are estimated from USDA FoodData Central cuttlefish cooked data as HPB does not report these; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed seafood dishes — analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate cooked seafood entries
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried cuttlefish with chilli. The University of Sydney GI Database lists cooked seafood and protein-dominant mixed dishes in the low GI range (GI 30–40). A GI of 32 is assigned as a conservative analogous estimate for this dish, which contains only 2.51 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as: GI (32) × available carbohydrate per serving (3.77 g at 150 g serving) / 100 = 1.2, confirming negligible glycaemic impact. This estimate is consistent with the low-carbohydrate, high-protein profile of the dish.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 503 mg per 100 g; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 755 mg sodium, representing over 30% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g or less, request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce during preparation, and avoid adding extra condiments; the naturally high cholesterol content of cuttlefish (~180 mg/100 g) also warrants portion control.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.51 g/100 g) and low sugar (2.41 g/100 g); high protein (11.5 g/100 g) supports glycaemic stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes at typical serving sizes due to negligible carbohydrate load and low GI (estimated 32); total meal glycaemic impact should be managed by monitoring accompanying rice or noodle portions rather than the cuttlefish dish itself.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 503 mg/100 g and cholesterol approximately 180 mg/100 g are the primary cardiovascular concerns; fat content (9.73 g/100 g) includes cooking oil used in stir-frying.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation (100 g or less per serving), request preparation with minimal oil and reduced-sodium sauces, and account for the cholesterol contribution of cuttlefish within their daily dietary cholesterol budget.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Chilli and ginger provide anti-inflammatory polyphenols (gingerols, capsaicin); however, sodium at 503 mg/100 g may worsen RA-related tissue swelling, and chilli is a nightshade vegetable.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients with nightshade sensitivity should monitor individual response to chilli; request reduced seasoning to lower sodium intake, which may otherwise exacerbate joint swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Cuttlefish with Chilli (辣炒墨鱼; Sotong Goreng Cili) is a popular Singaporean hawker and zi char dish consisting of fresh cuttlefish wok-fried at high heat with sliced onions, fresh or dried chilli, garlic, soy sauce, and oyster sauce. The cuttlefish is tender with a slightly chewy bite, and the sauce is savoury, mildly spicy, and umami-rich. Nutritionally, the dish provides 144 kcal per 100 g, with 11.5 g protein, 9.73 g fat (predominantly from cooking oil), and only 2.51 g carbohydrate, making it a low-carbohydrate, moderate-protein seafood dish. Sodium is elevated at 503 mg/100 g due to soy and oyster sauce, which is the primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. Cuttlefish is naturally high in cholesterol (~180 mg/100 g) and provides meaningful iron (2.07 mg/100 g) and calcium (61 mg/100 g). The dish is suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. Glycemic index is estimated at 32 (low GI) with a glycemic load of approximately 1.2 per 150 g serving, confirming negligible glycaemic impact. The dish is halal-certified in most hawker contexts and is dairy-free, though it contains mollusc allergens and soy-derived sauces.
common_user_queries: Is stir-fried cuttlefish with chilli healthy?
How many calories in sotong goreng cili?
Is cuttlefish with chilli suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat chilli cuttlefish if I have high blood pressure?
What is the protein content of stir-fried cuttlefish?
How much sodium is in chilli cuttlefish?
meal_context: served with fried rice or noodles

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database canonical entry for this exact dish (cr_id F03060035, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, potassium, and cholesterol estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked cuttlefish data as HPB does not report these; all adaptations documented. GI assigned as analogous estimate (GI 32, low) based on University of Sydney GI Database cooked seafood and protein-dominant dish references.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.5 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked cuttlefish (NDB 15149) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this dish; this is an adapted value. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for this stir-fried preparation is not available in HPB Singapore or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry. 3. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 (confirmed negligible/zero for this seafood stir-fry preparation; no trans fats expected from cuttlefish or standard cooking oils used). 4. potassium_mg (220 mg/100 g) and cholesterol_mg (180 mg/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked cuttlefish data as HPB does not report these micronutrients for this dish. 5. water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: these micronutrient values for this composite stir-fried dish are not available in the HPB Singapore database or USDA FoodData Central as a prepared dish entry as of 2026. 6. Glycemic index is assigned as 32 (low GI) based on analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database cooked seafood and protein-dominant mixed dish references; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. GL per 150 g serving = 32 × 3.77 / 100 = 1.2, confirming negligible glycaemic impact. 7. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 as reported by HPB; this is a confirmed zero from the primary source. 8. The dish is classified as Singaporean cuisine; all five mandatory multilingual names (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are present per G6 requirements.
